steps.title: Identify what Isla's method implies
steps.content: Isla specializes in the Nazca Lines yet was surprised he didn't recognize the whalelike geoglyph. He responded by using a drone to search a wide area from the air. The question asks what his aerial approach implies he thought about the geoglyph's location. If he could have found it on the ground through his normal fieldwork, he would already know it — so his drone search implies he believed the geoglyph is somewhere he had never been on the ground.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the match
steps.content:
• Choice A — must represent a species of whale that went extinct before there were any people in Peru.: Concerns the species depicted, not the geoglyph's location. An aerial search does nothing to clarify what animal a glyph represents. ✗
  • Choice B — is actually located in Germany, not Peru, and isn't part of the Nazca Lines at all.: The photograph was at a German exhibit, but the Nazca Lines are in Peru. Nothing in the text supports the glyph being in Germany. ✗
  • Choice C — is probably in a location Isla hadn't ever come across while on the ground.: Directly explains why aerial search makes sense — Isla's ground-level expertise had never covered that spot, so a wide aerial survey was needed. ✓
  • Choice D — was almost certainly created a long time after the other Nazca Lines geoglyphs were created.: Concerns the timeline of the glyph's creation. A drone search tells nothing about when something was made. ✗
That is the correct answer.
